sanskrit_devanagari: न मां दुष्कृतिनो मूढा: प्रपद्यन्ते नराधमा: ।माययापहृतज्ञाना आसुरं भावमाश्रिता: ॥ १५ ॥
sanskrit_iast: na māṁ duṣkṛtino mūḍhāḥprapadyante narādhamāḥmāyayāpahṛta-jñānāāsuraṁ bhāvam āśritāḥ

translations.verbatim_quote: Those miscreants who are grossly foolish, who are lowest among mankind, whose knowledge is stolen by illusion, and who partake of the atheistic nature of demons do not surrender unto Me.
Those miscreants who are grossly foolish, who are lowest among mankind, whose knowledge is stolen by illusion, and who partake of the atheistic nature of demons do not surrender unto Me.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt_status: captured (fair-use excerpt; full purport length: 11187 chars)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: It is said in Bhagavad-gītā that simply by surrendering oneself unto the lotus feet of the Supreme Personality Kṛṣṇa one can surmount the stringent laws of material nature. At this point a question arises: How is it that educated philosophers, scientists, businessmen, administrators and all the

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 7.15 Mudhah, the foolish; duskrtinah, evildoers, sinners; who are nara-adhamah, the most depraved among men; who are also apa-hrta-jnanah, deprived of, despoiled of (their) wisdom; mayaya, by Maya; and asritah, who resort to; asuram bhavam, demoniacal, ways, such as cruelty, untruthfulness, etc.;
commentaries.raw_full_path: data/sources/raw/bg-7-15-bgus.json (key: Commentary by Sri Adi Shankaracharya of Advaita Sampradaya:)
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: The foolish, the evildoers, the lowest among men whose knowledge is taken by māyā and who have accepted the asuric nature — these do not take refuge in Krishna. The verse names a structural class who, by the very nature of their inner state (māyā-stolen knowledge, asuric disposition), do not approach the Lord. Shankara reads this as descriptive, not prescriptive: the verse describes the structural pattern of who does not approach.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 7.15 ‘Evil-doers’, those who commit evil deeds, do not resort to Me. They are of four types, according to the degree of their evil deeds: (i) the foolish, (ii) the lowest of men, (iii) those persons deprived of knowledge by Maya, and (iv) those given to demoniac nature.
commentaries.raw_full_path: data/sources/raw/bg-7-15-bgus.json (key: Commentary by Sri Ramanuja of Sri Sampradaya:)
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Evildoers, those who commit evil deeds, do not resort to Krishna. They are described in the four parallel clauses: foolish, lowest among men, knowledge taken by māyā, of asuric nature. Each clause picks out a structural feature of the class who does not approach. Ramanuja's reading preserves the descriptive register: the verse names a pattern, not a moral indictment to be imitated.

positions.rendering: who partake of the atheistic nature of demons
those who have accepted the asuric (demonic) nature

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: The asuric-nature claim is the verse's most pointed and easiest-to-mishandle clause. All translators agree it names a structural disposition — not a fixed identity, but a settled state the person has accepted (āśritāḥ). Shankara's and Ramanuja's readings both preserve the descriptive register: the verse describes who does not approach, not who is to be excluded. Modern translators (Prabhupada, editorial) render it directly as 'demonic' or 'atheistic.' The chapter thesis explicitly warns: the engineering analog must not smuggle in a moral hierarchy of engineers. The verse's force at metaphysical level (some are not in the position to approach the Lord) does not license an engineering reading where the senior dismisses colleagues as 'asuric.' The structural pattern (some do not approach) is descriptive; the moral hierarchy is the misread.

literal_meaning: The evildoers, the deluded, the lowest of men, whose wisdom is taken by māyā, of asuric nature — these do not take refuge in me.
traditional_meaning_consensus: 7.15 names the class who, structurally, does not take refuge. Four parallel clauses describe the pattern: evildoers (duṣkṛtinaḥ), deluded (mūḍhāḥ), lowest of men (narādhamāḥ), knowledge stolen by māyā, asuric nature. Both Shankara and Ramanuja preserve the descriptive register — the verse describes a structural pattern (some are not in the position to approach), it does not license a moral indictment to be imitated. The verse's metaphysical force exceeds any engineering analog; the engineering layer must operate within the chapter thesis's explicit guard: do not smuggle a moral hierarchy of engineers into the analog. The structural pattern (some do not approach the substrate-discipline) maps; the moral hierarchy is the failure mode.
